hint: The basic building block of all matter.
The smallest structural and functional unit of an organism.
A measure of the amount of matter in an object.
A massive, luminous sphere of plasma held together by its own gravity.
The dense central region of a planet or star.
A unit of heredity transferred from a parent to offspring.
Thermal energy transferred between systems.
A chemical substance with a pH lower than 7.
A chemical substance with a pH higher than 7.
The force holding atoms together in a molecule.
Facts and statistics collected together for reference or analysis.
A metallic chemical element with the symbol Zn.
A noble gas commonly used in brightly colored glowing signs.
A strong, magnetic, silvery-gray metal.
A precious yellow metal highly valued in chemistry and electronics.
A heavy, dense, toxic metal with the chemical symbol Pb.
A natural satellite orbiting a planet.
The fourth planet from the Sun, known as the Red Planet.
A blood vessel that carries blood toward the heart.
The primary organ of the respiratory system in humans and many other animals.
A rigid organ that constitutes part of the vertebrate skeleton.
The largest organ covering the body of humans and other animals.
The upper layer of earth in which plants grow.
The perceptible natural movement of the air.
A naturally occurring solid mass or aggregate of minerals.
Moisture condensed from the atmosphere that falls visibly in separate drops.
The unit of reproduction of a flowering plant.
The part of a plant that attaches it to the ground and conveys water.
A warm-blooded egg-laying vertebrate characterized by feathers and wings.
A limbless cold-blooded vertebrate animal with gills and fins.
An infective agent that typically consists of a nucleic acid molecule in a protein coat.
The curved trajectory of an object, such as a planet, around a star.
A substance that has no fixed shape and yields easily to external pressure.
A push or pull upon an object resulting from its interaction with another object.
The natural agent that stimulates sight and makes things visible.
Vibrations that travel through the air or another medium and can be heard.
The boundless three-dimensional extent in which objects and events have relative position.
A celestial object consisting of a nucleus of ice and dust, featuring a 'tail' when near the sun.
A device that generates an intense beam of coherent monochromatic light.
A system for detecting the presence, direction, distance, and speed of aircraft or ships.
A system for the detection of objects under water by emitting sound pulses.
An organism or cell produced asexually from one ancestor, to which it is genetically identical.
The plants of a particular region, habitat, or geological period.
The animals of a particular region, habitat, or geological period.
An organ that synthesizes substances for release into the bloodstream or into cavities.
The red liquid that circulates in the arteries and veins of humans and other vertebrates.
The organ serving as the center of the nervous system in vertebrate and invertebrate animals.
A rhythmical throbbing of the arteries as blood is propelled through them.
A whitish fiber that transmits impulses of sensation to the brain or spinal cord.
The bone framework enclosing the brain of a vertebrate.
The quantitative property that must be transferred to a body or physical system to perform work.
Any substance that has mass and takes up space by having volume.
A subatomic particle with a positive electric charge found in the nucleus of an atom.
The chemical element of atomic number 6, forming the basis of all known life.
A colorless, odorless reactive gas that forms about 21 percent of the earth's atmosphere.
An ensemble of similar cells and their extracellular matrix from the same origin.
A gravitationally bound system of stars, stellar remnants, interstellar gas, dust, and dark matter.
An astronomical body orbiting a star or stellar remnant.
The preserved remains or traces of a dead organism.
One of the four fundamental states of matter, consisting of a gas of ions and free electrons.
A nearly incompressible fluid that conforms to the shape of its container.
Space devoid of matter.
A material or object that produces a magnetic field.
A soft, malleable, and ductile metal with very high thermal and electrical conductivity.
A precious shiny grayish-white metal, the chemical element of atomic number 47.
A colorless, odorless, tasteless, non-toxic, inert, monatomic gas.
A distinct body of interstellar clouds, which can be a nursery for new stars.
A streak of light in the sky caused by a meteoroid crashing into Earth's atmosphere.
The scientific study of plants.
A substance produced by a living organism which acts as a catalyst to bring about a specific biochemical reaction.
A living organism that feeds on organic matter, typically having specialized sense organs and nervous system.
A warm-blooded vertebrate animal distinguished by the possession of hair or fur.
A solid material whose constituents are arranged in a highly ordered microscopic structure.
The dense, positively charged center of an atom.
A subatomic particle of about the same mass as a proton but without an electric charge.
The natural phenomenon by which all things with mass or energy are brought toward one another.
The natural science that studies life and living organisms.
The natural science that studies matter, its motion and behavior through space and time.
The earth science concerned with the solid Earth, the rocks of which it is composed, and the processes by which they change.
The branch of science concerned with the bodily structure of humans, animals, and other living organisms.
The branch of biology that deals with the relations of organisms to one another and to their physical surroundings.
The scientific study of the behavior, structure, physiology, classification, and distribution of animals.
The state of the atmosphere at a place and time as regards heat, dryness, sunshine, wind, rain, etc.
The weather conditions prevailing in an area in general or over a long period.
A rupture in the crust of a planetary-mass object that allows hot lava, volcanic ash, and gases to escape.
A solid inorganic substance of natural occurrence.
A species of atom having the same number of protons in its atomic nucleus.
The basic unit of classification and a taxonomic rank of an organism.
The natural home or environment of an animal, plant, or other organism.
A substance that has a molecular structure consisting chiefly or entirely of a large number of similar units bonded together.
Relating to or resulting from motion.
Relating to heat.
Relating to sight, especially in relation to the physical action of light.
A process by which molecules of a solvent tend to pass through a semipermeable membrane.
A stable subatomic particle with a charge of negative electricity, found in all atoms.
A group of atoms bonded together, representing the smallest fundamental unit of a chemical compound.
A process that involves rearrangement of the molecular or ionic structure of a substance.
The resistance that one surface or object encounters when moving over another.
The speed of something in a given direction.
The quantity of motion of a moving body, measured as a product of its mass and velocity.
The scientific discipline involved with elements and compounds composed of atoms, molecules and ions.
The natural science that studies celestial objects and phenomena.
Change in the heritable characteristics of biological populations over successive generations.
The emission of energy as electromagnetic waves or as moving subatomic particles.
The production of chemical compounds by reaction from simpler materials.
A biological community of interacting organisms and their physical environment.
The worldwide sum of all ecosystems, also known as the ecosphere.
An artificial body placed in orbit around the earth or moon or another planet.
An optical instrument designed to make distant objects appear nearer.
An optical instrument used for viewing very small objects.
A scientific procedure undertaken to make a discovery, test a hypothesis, or demonstrate a known fact.
A proposed explanation made on the basis of limited evidence as a starting point for further investigation.
A room or building equipped for scientific experiments, research, or teaching.
The degree or intensity of heat present in a substance or object.
A form of energy resulting from the existence of charged particles.
A physical phenomenon produced by the motion of electric charge, resulting in attractive and repulsive forces.
A threadlike structure of nucleic acids and protein found in the nucleus of most living cells.
The chemical processes that occur within a living organism in order to maintain life.
The process by which green plants and some other organisms use sunlight to synthesize foods.
The variety of plant and animal life in the world or in a particular habitat.
An animal of a large group distinguished by the possession of a backbone or spinal column.
An animal lacking a backbone, such as an arthropod, mollusk, or annelid.
A cold-blooded vertebrate animal of a class that comprises the frogs, toads, newts, and salamanders.
An animal that feeds on flesh.
An animal that feeds on plants.
A green pigment, present in all green plants and in cyanobacteria, responsible for the absorption of light.
A plastid that contains chlorophyll and in which photosynthesis takes place.
An organelle found in large numbers in most cells, in which the biochemical processes of respiration and energy production occur.
A compound consisting of a nucleoside linked to a phosphate group, forming the basic structural unit of nucleic acids.
Any of a large group of organic compounds occurring in foods and living tissues and including sugars, starch, and cellulose.
The envelope of gases surrounding the earth or another planet.
The layer of the earth's atmosphere above the troposphere, extending to about 32 miles above the earth's surface.
The lowest region of the atmosphere, extending from the earth's surface to a height of about 3.7–6.2 miles.
The rigid outer part of the earth, consisting of the crust and upper mantle.
All the waters on the earth's surface, such as lakes and seas, and sometimes including water over the earth's surface.
A sudden and violent shaking of the ground, sometimes causing great destruction.
A storm with a violent wind, in particular a tropical cyclone in the Caribbean.
A star that suddenly increases greatly in brightness because of a catastrophic explosion that ejects most of its mass.
A meteor that survives its passage through the earth's atmosphere such that part of it strikes the ground.
A planet that orbits a star outside the solar system.
A room or building housing an astronomical telescope or other scientific equipment.
Emitting or relating to the emission of ionizing radiation or particles.
Electromagnetic radiation with a wavelength from 10 nm to 400 nm, shorter than that of visible light.
An electromagnetic wave with a wavelength in the range 0.001–0.3 m, shorter than that of a normal radio wave.
The distance between successive crests of a wave, especially points in a sound wave or electromagnetic wave.
The rate at which a vibration occurs that constitutes a wave.
The branch of physical science that deals with the relations between heat and other forms of energy.
The study of the properties of moving air, and especially of the interaction between the air and solid bodies moving through it.
The branch of science concerned with fossil animals and plants.
The action of purifying a liquid by a process of heating and cooling.
